section.title: The Call of Jeremiah
section.sectionRationale: This is the book's opening seam: before any oracle of judgment is spoken, the LORD establishes who is speaking and why he can be trusted. A priest's son from Anathoth is claimed before birth, commissioned despite his fear, and armed with two visions and a promise of protection, setting the terms for the long, hard ministry that follows.
section.thesis: The LORD forms, consecrates, and appoints Jeremiah as a prophet before his birth, and though Jeremiah feels only youth and fear, God's own presence and watchful word make him as unmovable as a fortified city against the kings, priests, and people who will oppose him.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the prophet introduced, priest and son of Judah's last kings; the call itself, formed and set apart before birth; two confirming visions, the almond branch and the boiling pot from the north; and the commission to stand, fortified against all opposition.

units.label: Introducing Jeremiah
units.note: a priest of Anathoth, spanning the last kings of Judah

verses.text: These are the words of Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, one of the priests in Anathoth in the territory of Benjamin.
The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah in the thirteenth year of the reign of Josiah son of Amon king of Judah,
and through the days of Jehoiakim son of Josiah king of Judah, until the fifth month of the eleventh year of Zedekiah son of Josiah king of Judah, when the people of Jerusalem went into exile.
The word of the LORD came to me, saying:

“Before I {{yatsar|formed}} you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I {{qadash|set you apart}} 
and appointed you as a prophet to the nations.”
“Ah, Lord GOD,” I said, “I surely do not know how to speak, for I am only a child!”

But the LORD told me: 
“Do not say, ‘I am only a child.’ For to everyone I send you, you must go, and all that I command you, you must speak.
Do not be afraid of them, for I am with you to deliver you,” declares the LORD.

Then the LORD reached out His hand, touched my mouth, and said to me: 
“Behold, I have put My words in your mouth.

See, I have appointed you today over nations and kingdoms to uproot and tear down, to destroy and overthrow, 
to build and plant.”

units.label: Two confirming visions
units.note: the almond branch and the boiling pot

verses.text:
And the word of the LORD came to me, asking, “Jeremiah, what do you see?” 
“I see a branch of an {{shaqed|almond}} tree,” I replied.
“You have observed correctly,” said the LORD, “for I am watching over My word to accomplish it.”

Again the word of the LORD came to me, asking, “What do you see?” 
“I see a boiling pot,” I replied, “and it is tilting toward us from the north.”
Then the LORD said to me, “Disaster from the north will be poured out on all who live in the land.

For I am about to summon all the clans and kingdoms of the north,” declares the LORD. 
“Their kings will come and set up their thrones at the entrance of the gates of Jerusalem. They will attack all her surrounding walls and all the other cities of Judah.

I will pronounce My judgments against them for all their wickedness, because they have forsaken Me to burn incense to other gods 
and to worship the works of their own hands.
Get yourself ready. Stand up and tell them everything that I command you. Do not be intimidated by them, or I will terrify you before them.

Now behold, this day I have made you like a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls against the whole land, against the kings of Judah, its officials, its priests, and the people of the land.
They will fight against you but will never overcome you, since I am with you to deliver you,” declares the LORD.

yatsar.gloss: To form or shape, the same verb used of the LORD forming man from dust in Genesis 2:7. Before Jeremiah drew a single breath, God was already the potter shaping this particular life for a particular purpose.

qadash.gloss: To set apart, consecrate, make holy for a specific use. Jeremiah's whole life, not merely his message, is marked out before birth as belonging to God's purpose, the same word used of priests and vessels devoted to the LORD's service.

shaqed.gloss: Almond, from a root meaning to watch or wake, since the almond tree is the first to bloom in late winter. The wordplay is deliberate, God shows Jeremiah an almond branch, shaqed, and answers, I am shoqed, watching, over My word to perform it.

basic.context: Jeremiah ministers to Judah across roughly four decades, from good King Josiah's reforms through the disastrous reigns that follow, right up to Jerusalem's fall and exile. Before any of the hard words to come, God tells Jeremiah plainly who he is and who has sent him: chosen before birth, appointed prophet to the nations, and promised protection despite fierce opposition from his own people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical call narrative, not merely a literary device, and it explains why Jeremiah could keep speaking an unwelcome message for forty years without being silenced. The honest hard question is verse 5, before you were born I set you apart, which raises real questions about God's sovereign choice over a life before that person has done anything; the text itself does not resolve every philosophical question this raises, but it grounds Jeremiah's whole ministry in a purpose fixed before his own choices began.
basic.originalLanguages: God formed, yatsar, Jeremiah in the womb, the same verb used of forming Adam from the dust, and set him apart, qadash, the word used elsewhere of consecrating priests and holy things. The almond branch, shaqed, plays on the Hebrew word for watching, shoqed, God's pledge that He is watching over His word to bring it to pass.
basic.typology: Jeremiah, formed and appointed before birth, foreshadows Christ, who was likewise sent from the Father before the foundation of the world for a specific mission, and who, like Jeremiah, was a prophet rejected by His own people. Jeremiah's promised protection, a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls, anticipates the greater and more costly faithfulness of Jesus, who was not delivered from death but through it.
basic.christ: Paul echoes this call in describing his own calling, set apart from my mother's womb, Galatians 1:15, showing that God's pattern of pre-birth calling extends into the New Testament and ultimately points to the Son, sent into the world before the world's foundation for the work of redemption, John 1:1 and Ephesians 1:4. Jeremiah's lifelong rejection by kings, priests, and people previews the greater rejection of the true Prophet, Jesus, who also stood alone and unmoved against every hostile power.
basic.god: God is sovereign over a life before it is lived, choosing and appointing Jeremiah for a purpose Jeremiah did not choose for himself. Yet God is also patient and near: He meets Jeremiah's fear not with rebuke but with reassurance, His own presence, His own words placed in the prophet's mouth, and His own promise, I am with you to deliver you.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 1:15 has Paul use nearly identical language of his own calling before birth. Exodus 4:10-12 shows Moses raising the same objection of inadequate speech, answered the same way. Isaiah 49:1-5 has the Servant of the LORD likewise called from the womb. Ezekiel 2:6 and 3:8-9 give a prophet the same command not to fear and the same promise of a face like flint.
basic.oneStory: This is the commissioning of the prophet whose forty years of ministry will carry Judah from the last days of faithful kingship through covenant unfaithfulness, siege, and exile, into the first clear promise of a new covenant. The pattern set here, chosen before birth, sent despite weakness, sustained only by God's presence, runs through Scripture to its fullest expression in Christ, the Prophet who was set apart before the world began and who was not overcome by those who opposed Him.

context.literary: The book opens with a formal superscription (1:1-3) locating Jeremiah's ministry across five kings, then moves into a classic prophetic call narrative: divine address, human objection, divine reassurance, and commissioning, a pattern shared with Moses, Isaiah, and Ezekiel. The two visions in verses 11-16 function as a hinge, confirming both the certainty of God's word and the specific content of the coming judgment.
context.historical: Jeremiah's call falls in 627 BC, the thirteenth year of the reforming King Josiah, a high point of hope for covenant renewal. Yet Jeremiah's ministry would outlast that hope, running through Jehoiakim's apostasy and Zedekiah's weak vacillation, to the Babylonian conquest and exile in 586 BC, so this call anticipates decades of resistance the young Jeremiah could not yet imagine.
context.canonical: As the opening chapter of the longest prophetic book in the Old Testament, this call narrative sets the theological terms, sovereign choice, human inadequacy, divine presence, for reading everything that follows, including Jeremiah's own later complaints against the very calling described here.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish Jeremiah's divine authorization at the outset, so that the harsh judgments and unwelcome demands that follow are heard as the LORD's own word rather than merely one man's opinion, and to show that God equips whom He calls despite their sense of inadequacy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a unique, historical commissioning of one specific prophet; it does not prescribe that every believer will receive an identical audible call with visions. The enduring, prescriptive truths are that God's purposes for His people precede their own choices, that felt inadequacy is no excuse to refuse God's call, and that God's presence, not human confidence, is what sustains costly obedience.

debates.question: Does verse 5 teach that Jeremiah's specific individual vocation was fixed before birth, or does it speak more broadly of God's sovereign knowledge and purpose for him?

views.label: A specific, individual pre-birth calling
views.case: The verb sequence, formed, knew, set apart, appointed, reads as a deliberate, particular assignment to the office of prophet, parallel to Isaiah 49:1 and Galatians 1:15, and it is this specificity that grounds Jeremiah's confidence under later opposition.

views.label: A statement of God's sovereign foreknowledge more broadly applied
views.case: Some read this as covenantal language emphasizing God's intimate prior knowledge and purposeful claim on Jeremiah's life, without necessarily settling every question of individual predestination that later theological debates would raise.

debates.bearing: Either reading grounds the same pastoral point: Jeremiah's authority and endurance rest on a purpose God set in motion before Jeremiah had any part in it, not on Jeremiah's own aptitude or willingness.

study.originalLanguages: God formed, yatsar, Jeremiah in the womb, the same verb used of shaping Adam from the dust in Genesis 2:7, and set him apart, qadash, language elsewhere used of consecrating priests and holy vessels to the LORD's exclusive use. The almond branch, shaqed, is a deliberate wordplay on shoqed, watching, God's assurance that He is watching over His word to perform it, a pledge borne out across every chapter that follows.

typology.type: Jeremiah formed and set apart before birth, Jeremiah 1:5
typology.fulfillment: Christ, sent from the Father before the foundation of the world, Ephesians 1:4, and Paul's own calling from the womb, Galatians 1:15, which extends this pattern into apostolic ministry.

typology.type: Jeremiah made a fortified city, iron pillar, and bronze walls against opposition, Jeremiah 1:18-19
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who stood unmoved against the combined hostility of religious leaders, crowds, and rulers, and who, unlike Jeremiah, was delivered through death itself rather than merely from it.

study.christ: Paul deliberately echoes this call in Galatians 1:15, set apart from my mother's womb, showing that the pattern of sovereign, pre-birth calling is not unique to Jeremiah but belongs to God's larger way of raising up servants, ultimately fulfilled in the Son who was appointed before the world's foundation for the work of redemption. Jeremiah's lifelong rejection by the very kings, priests, and people he was sent to, and his preservation through it, previews the greater rejection and greater vindication of Jesus, the Prophet like Moses who was refused by His own yet triumphed over every hostile power.
study.god: God is sovereign over Jeremiah's life before Jeremiah had lived a single day of it, and He is equally near in the moment Jeremiah feels most inadequate, answering fear not with rebuke but with His own hand touching Jeremiah's mouth and His own presence pledged for deliverance. His justice against the coming disaster from the north stands alongside His patient commitment to equip and protect the messenger who must announce it.

crossReferences.note: Paul describes his own apostolic call in language that directly echoes Jeremiah's, set apart from my mother's womb.
Moses objects that he cannot speak well, and the LORD answers, as with Jeremiah, by promising His own presence and words.
The Servant of the LORD is likewise called and named from the womb for a mission to the nations.
Ezekiel receives the same command not to fear his hearers and the same promise of being made hard as flint against opposition.

study.oneStory: This is the commissioning that launches Jeremiah into forty years of hard, mostly unwelcome ministry, from Josiah's reforms through the fall of Jerusalem. The pattern set here, a life claimed and shaped before birth, a fearful servant reassured by God's own presence, a word that will not fail, runs through the whole of Scripture and comes to rest in Christ, the Prophet appointed before the foundation of the world, rejected by His own, yet never overcome.

sermon.title: Chosen, Called, and Faithful
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly through Jeremiah 1:1-19, drawing out God's sovereign claim on a life before birth, the equipping of a fearful servant, and the confidence that comes from knowing God preserves those He appoints, exactly the movement this study traces.

quiz.q: What does God say about Jeremiah before he was even born?

options.text: That He formed him, knew him, set him apart, and appointed him a prophet to the nations.
options.note: Verse 5 grounds Jeremiah's entire ministry in a purpose fixed before his birth.

options.text: That he would become a priest like his father Hilkiah.
options.note: Jeremiah was from a priestly family, but God's specific word appoints him a prophet, not a priest.

options.text: That he would live a long and comfortable life.
options.note: The chapter promises deliverance amid conflict, not comfort or ease.

options.text: Nothing; the call came only as an adult.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly speaks of God's purpose before Jeremiah was formed in the womb.

quiz.q: How does Jeremiah initially respond to his call?

options.text: With an objection that he does not know how to speak and is only a child.
options.note: Verse 6 records Jeremiah's honest sense of inadequacy, met by God's reassurance rather than rebuke.

options.text: With immediate, confident acceptance and no hesitation.
options.note: Jeremiah does ultimately serve faithfully, but his first response is hesitant, not confident.

options.text: By refusing outright and fleeing the country.
options.note: Jeremiah raises an objection but does not refuse or flee; he goes on to serve for decades.

options.text: By demanding a sign before he would believe God.
options.note: His objection is about his own inadequacy, not a demand for proof.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the almond branch vision in verses 11-12?

options.text: It plays on the Hebrew words for almond and watching, confirming God is watching over His word to fulfill it.
options.note: Shaqed, almond, and shoqed, watching, form a deliberate wordplay assuring Jeremiah that God's word will not fail.

options.text: It predicts a literal famine caused by a lack of almonds.
options.note: The vision is about God's watchfulness over His word, not agricultural conditions.

options.text: It is simply a random object with no theological meaning.
options.note: The vision carries clear theological weight through its wordplay.

options.text: It symbolizes the sweetness of the message Jeremiah will preach.
options.note: Jeremiah's message will be largely one of judgment, not sweetness; the vision's point is God's watchfulness.

quiz.q: What promise does God give Jeremiah to sustain him against opposition?

options.text: That He will make Jeremiah like a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls, and be with him to deliver him.
options.note: Verses 18-19 promise not the absence of conflict but God's presence and preservation within it.

options.text: That no one in Judah will ever oppose him.
options.note: The text explicitly says kings, officials, priests, and people will fight against him.

options.text: That his ministry will be short and painless.
options.note: Jeremiah's ministry spans roughly forty years and is marked by suffering, not ease.

options.text: That he will be given political power over Judah's kings.
options.note: The image of nations and kingdoms in verse 10 is about his prophetic word's authority, not political rule.

quiz.q: How does Jeremiah's call anticipate the New Testament?

options.text: Paul describes his own apostolic calling in nearly identical language, set apart from his mother's womb.
options.note: Galatians 1:15 deliberately echoes Jeremiah 1:5, showing the same pattern of pre-birth calling continuing into apostolic ministry.

options.text: It has no connection to anything in the New Testament.
options.note: Paul's language in Galatians 1:15 directly echoes this passage.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Christ's birth.
options.note: The passage speaks of calling and purpose, not chronological prediction.

options.text: It shows that all New Testament believers receive audible calls with visions.
options.note: The pattern of purposeful calling extends to Paul, but Scripture does not generalize identical audible calls and visions to all believers.

questions: God says He knew and set Jeremiah apart before he was born. How does knowing your life has purpose, even before you could choose anything, change how you face what God calls you to now?
Jeremiah's honest objection, I do not know how to speak, for I am only a child, was met with reassurance rather than rejection. Where are you hiding from a calling because you feel unqualified?
God's answer to Jeremiah's fear was not a change of circumstance but a promise of presence, I am with you to deliver you. Where do you need that same promise more than a change in your situation?

The almond branch reminded Jeremiah that God is watching over His word to perform it. What promise of God do you need to trust is being watched over and brought to pass, even though you cannot yet see it?
God made Jeremiah a fortified city against real opposition, not an easy road. Where might God be calling you to stand firm rather than promising you an easier path?

translationNotes.note: Set apart translates a Hebrew verb, qadash, elsewhere rendered consecrated or sanctified; some English versions render it consecrated, but the underlying idea, set apart wholly for God's use, is the same.
The wordplay between almond, shaqed, and watching, shoqed, is audible in Hebrew but invisible in English translation; the BSB footnote or study notes typically flag this pun.

Fortified city, iron pillar, and bronze walls are three distinct images of unbreakable strength; some translations render the second as pillar of iron, but the sense of impregnable defense is the same across versions.

meta.notes: BSB text and sermon links fetched and verified via the Makor scripture toolkit and live web search this session.
